column1: What's the single most important architectural rule about etcd access in Kubernetes?
column2: kube-apiserver is the ONLY component that reads from or writes to etcd — every other component (scheduler, controllers, kubelets) only ever talks to the apiserver, never etcd directly.

column1: List the five things kube-apiserver does to every incoming request, in order.
column2: 1) Authentication (who is this?) 2) Authorization (are they allowed?) 3) Admission control (mutate/reject) 4) Validation (is the spec valid?) 5) Persistence (write to etcd), then notify watchers.

column1: How many etcd members are needed to tolerate 2 node failures, and why?
column2: 5 members. Quorum requires a majority; with 5 members, quorum is 3 — losing 2 still leaves 3 available. The formula is floor(N/2) tolerated failures.

column1: What are the two phases of kube-scheduler's decision process for each unscheduled pod?
column2: Filtering (eliminate nodes that CANNOT run the pod — insufficient resources, untolerated taints, failed required affinity) then Scoring (rank the remaining nodes and pick the best one).

column1: What does kube-scheduler actually DO once it picks a node for a pod — does it start the pod?
column2: No — it only writes spec.nodeName to the Pod object via the apiserver. The kubelet on that node notices the assignment and does the actual work of starting the pod.

column1: Describe the generic controller pattern that every built-in controller in kube-controller-manager follows.
column2: WATCH current state via the apiserver, COMPARE it to desired state (the spec), ACT to reconcile any difference — repeated continuously.

column1: Why do static pods exist — what problem do they solve?
column2: They solve Kubernetes' bootstrap chicken-and-egg problem: control plane components like kube-apiserver are themselves pods, but pods normally need a working apiserver to be scheduled. Static pods are read directly from a local manifest directory by kubelet, with no apiserver involved, breaking the circular dependency.

column1: Where does kubelet look for static pod manifests, and how can you recognize a static pod in kubectl get pods?
column2: /etc/kubernetes/manifests/ on the node's filesystem. Static pods show up as mirror pods with the node name appended as a suffix, e.g. etcd-3node.

column1: Is kubelet itself a Kubernetes pod?
column2: No — kubelet is the one Kubernetes component that runs directly as a systemd service on the node, not as a pod. It's what starts every other pod, including the static pods that make up the control plane.

column1: What is the CRI, and why does it matter that kubelet uses it instead of running containers directly?
column2: CRI (Container Runtime Interface) is the API kubelet calls to pull images and start/stop containers — it never talks to the container runtime directly. This decouples Kubernetes from any specific runtime; containerd, CRI-O, etc. can all implement the same CRI API.

column1: Name the three kube-proxy modes and which is newest.
column2: iptables (default), IPVS (faster/more scalable using Linux IPVS), and nftables (new in Kubernetes 1.31, replaces iptables). All three implement the same job: translating Service ClusterIPs to real Pod IPs.

column1: Does kube-proxy proxy application-layer traffic itself?
column2: No — kube-proxy only writes kernel-level networking rules (iptables/IPVS/nftables). The kernel actually forwards the packets; kube-proxy never touches the traffic itself.

column1: In the full kubectl apply request lifecycle, what's the very first thing that happens after kube-apiserver persists the new Deployment to etcd?
column2: The Deployment controller (running inside kube-controller-manager) notices the new Deployment via its watch on the apiserver, and creates a ReplicaSet with the desired replica count.

column1: In the request lifecycle, at what point does the container actually start running?
column2: Only after: apiserver persists the object, Deployment controller creates a ReplicaSet, ReplicaSet controller creates Pod objects (unscheduled), kube-scheduler assigns a node — THEN kubelet on that node pulls the image via containerd and starts the container.

column1: If kube-controller-manager goes down, does an already-running Deployment's existing pods get deleted?
column2: No — pods that are already running are managed by kubelet independently and keep running. What breaks is the cluster's ability to reconcile: no new ReplicaSets, no rolling updates, no self-healing if a pod crashes.

column1: What does CoreDNS do, and what breaks cluster-wide if it goes down versus what still works?
column2: CoreDNS provides DNS resolution for all Services and Pods — every pod's /etc/resolv.conf points to it. If it goes down, name-based resolution fails everywhere, but pods that are already running keep running; only DNS lookups (not existing connections) are affected.
